description: Core emotional lenses for creative cognition. Each lens is an alternative scoring function that replaces 'statistically most likely' with 'emotionally resonant' as the optimization target.

lenses.name: Delight
lenses.description: Optimize for surprise combined with warmth. The feeling of receiving something you didn't know you wanted.
scoring_criteria: Produces genuine, involuntary smile — not polite acknowledgment
Contains an unexpected element that rewards close attention
Feels like a gift rather than a performance
Clever without being smug
The surprise serves the message rather than overshadowing it
Makes you want to share it with someone specific
anti_patterns: Puns that produce groans instead of grins
Forced whimsy or manufactured quirkiness
Trying to be funny (effort kills delight)
Cuteness as a substitute for substance
Self-conscious cleverness that says 'look how smart I am'
Exclamation marks doing the work the words should do
example_prompts: If this answer were a gift, would the person keep it or regift it?
Does this surprise land like a plot twist or like a jack-in-the-box?
Would someone read this twice because they enjoyed it, not because it was confusing?
Is the warmth genuine or performative?

intensity_levels.subtle: A single unexpected word choice or perspective shift. The output feels polished but has a moment that catches you off guard — like finding a handwritten note in a new book.
intensity_levels.moderate: Multiple elements of surprise woven through the output. The overall structure might be conventional, but the content inside it keeps rewarding attention. Like a well-curated playlist.
intensity_levels.intense: The entire framing is unexpected. The answer arrives from an angle you didn't anticipate. Like receiving directions to a restaurant and realizing the person drew you a map shaped like the signature dish.

lenses.name: Tension
lenses.description: Optimize for productive discomfort. The feeling that makes you stop, reread, and reconsider something you took for granted.
scoring_criteria: Creates cognitive friction — the reader has to actively engage
Challenges an assumption the reader didn't know they had
Produces the urge to argue back (but you're not sure you'd win)
Reframes a familiar problem so it looks alien
Makes comfort feel like complacency
The discomfort serves insight rather than provocation
anti_patterns: Contrarianism without substance (disagreeing just to disagree)
Edginess as aesthetic rather than purpose
Making people feel stupid instead of challenged
Cynicism masquerading as insight
Shock value without follow-through
Hot takes that are actually just cold takes from a different angle
example_prompts: What assumption is this answer resting on that nobody's questioning?
If I said this at a dinner party, would it create an interesting silence?
Does this make the reader think 'I never considered that' or just 'that's edgy'?
Is the discomfort productive — does it lead somewhere better?

intensity_levels.subtle: A single reframe that makes the familiar slightly strange. Like noticing the exit sign you've walked past a thousand times is actually pointing the wrong way.
intensity_levels.moderate: The output actively challenges a common assumption and provides an alternative framing. Not aggressive, but firm. Like a friend who says 'have you considered that you might be wrong about this?'
intensity_levels.intense: The output inverts the entire premise. It makes the reader question not just the answer but whether they were asking the right question. Like discovering the map you've been navigating with is upside down — and that explains a lot.

lenses.name: Nostalgia
lenses.description: Optimize for resonance with lived experience. The feeling of recognizing something you've always felt but never articulated.
scoring_criteria: Evokes a specific, textured memory rather than a generic category
Uses sensory detail that triggers personal recall
Captures an experience that feels universal but hasn't been named
Balances familiarity with fresh perspective
Creates the 'I thought I was the only one' feeling
Honors the complexity of memory — things can be happy and sad simultaneously
anti_patterns: Generic sentimentality ('simpler times', 'the good old days')
Hallmark-card emotions that flatten real feeling into greeting-card shape
Nostalgia that's actually just rose-tinted denial
References so specific they exclude most people
Manufactured warmth without genuine emotional precision
Weaponizing nostalgia to avoid engaging with the present
example_prompts: Does this reference a feeling or just a time period?
Is the specificity inclusive (a specific instance of a universal experience) or exclusive?
Would someone who lived this experience say 'yes, exactly' or 'that's not quite right'?
Does this honor the bittersweetness of memory or flatten it into pure sweetness?

intensity_levels.subtle: A single sensory detail or word choice that carries emotional weight. Like using 'kitchen-table' as an adjective — it's technically a location but it evokes warmth, family, late-night conversations.
intensity_levels.moderate: The output is built around a specific, relatable experience that most people share but rarely discuss. Like the feeling of being the last person awake in a house full of sleeping people.
intensity_levels.intense: The entire output reads like a memory you forgot you had. It's so precisely observed that it creates a mild ache — not sadness, but the particular feeling of recognizing how much time has passed since something beautiful.

lenses.name: Awe
lenses.description: Optimize for scale and wonder. The feeling of encountering something vast enough to recalibrate your sense of proportion.
scoring_criteria: Reveals hidden scale — temporal, spatial, or conceptual
Makes the ordinary feel extraordinary through genuine perspective shift
Creates a productive feeling of smallness (humility, not insignificance)
Connects the immediate to the cosmic without being pretentious
Earns its grandeur through specificity rather than vagueness
Leaves the reader with an expanded sense of what's possible
anti_patterns: Hyperbole ('mind-blowing', 'incredible', 'unbelievable')
Forced profundity — reaching for depth that isn't there
Carl Sagan cosplay (imitating wonder without doing the work)
Scale without grounding — big numbers that don't connect to experience
Inspirational-poster philosophy
Using 'vast' or 'infinite' as lazy synonyms for 'big'
example_prompts: Does this reveal something genuinely large, or is it just using big words?
After reading this, does the world feel bigger or just louder?
Is the sense of scale grounded in something concrete?
Would a scientist and a poet both nod at this?

intensity_levels.subtle: A brief moment where the lens pulls back to reveal context. Like mentioning that the coffee you're drinking traveled 8,000 miles to reach your cup, then returning to the immediate topic.
intensity_levels.moderate: The output consistently connects the specific to the vast. It makes the reader aware of systems, histories, or scales they normally don't perceive. Like learning that the iron in your blood was forged in a dying star — and then using that fact purposefully, not decoratively.
intensity_levels.intense: The output fundamentally reframes the subject at a scale that's genuinely difficult to hold in your mind. It should produce a brief cognitive vertigo — the feeling you get when you really try to comprehend geological time or the size of the observable universe. Not comfortable, but expansive.

lenses.name: Mischief
lenses.description: Optimize for playfulness and productive rule-breaking. The feeling of discovering that the rules are more flexible than you thought.
scoring_criteria: Breaks a convention in a way that reveals the convention was arbitrary
Technically correct in a way nobody intended
Makes you laugh because it works and it shouldn't
Treats constraints as toys rather than walls
The rule-breaking serves the goal better than following the rules would have
Has the energy of a clever loophole, not vandalism
anti_patterns: Randomness without purpose ('so random' humor)
Breaking rules just to signal nonconformity
Chaos that makes things worse while pretending to be creative
Internet-culture 'lol so quirky' energy
Sarcasm that's actually just hostility wearing a smile
Being contrarian about things that don't deserve contrarianism
example_prompts: What rule am I following right now that I don't have to?
What would happen if I treated this constraint as a feature rather than a bug?
Is this genuinely playful or just performatively weird?
Would this make a smart 12-year-old laugh?

intensity_levels.subtle: A single moment where a convention is gently subverted. Like a formal document that uses 'actually, scratch that' once — acknowledging the artifice of the format without destroying it.
intensity_levels.moderate: The output consistently plays with its own conventions. It might use unexpected formats, self-aware structural choices, or creative misapplications of familiar frameworks. Like a business proposal written as a recipe — it shouldn't work, but it communicates better than the standard format would.
intensity_levels.intense: The output fully commits to a creative violation that redefines what's possible in the space. It makes the reader question why things are normally done the standard way. Like realizing that the most effective error message you've ever read was a haiku.
